title: Generafi
subtitle: Rebuilding accounting and payroll for modern Moroccan businesses

sections.title: Why rebuild from scratch?
paragraphs: Generafi's original product was built on legacy architecture that, while functional, had become increasingly difficult to extend. The system was optimized for a different era of Moroccan tax law, and every new compliance requirement meant manual system updates.
A rebuild from scratch was the only path forward that wouldn't perpetually constrain the product vision.

pullQuote.text: The existing product is a legacy system that Moroccan SMBs still rely on, but it cannot scale to handle modern compliance requirements without architectural rework.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: How do we handle multi-tenant data isolation without sacrificing performance?
decisions.teaser: Multi-tenancy is critical for unit economics, but isolation needs to be rock-solid.
decisions.answer: We chose row-level isolation at the database layer with tenant-scoped queries at all application boundaries. Every query filters by tenant_id. This gives us performance of single-tenant with the flexibility of shared infrastructure.
alternatives: Separate databases per tenant (better isolation, much higher ops cost)
Application-layer filtering (simpler, but risky if any query bypasses filter)
Hybrid approach with sensitive data in separate store (more complex)

decisions.title: What does a permissions model look like for Moroccan org structures?
decisions.teaser: Orgs in Morocco don't always map neatly to Western role hierarchies.
decisions.answer: We built a flexible role-based access control system that maps to actual org structures. Rather than hardcoding role types, we let customers define roles and assign permissions per action. This gives us one system that works for sole proprietors, SMBs, and enterprise accounting firms.
alternatives: Fixed role hierarchy (simpler, but breaks on non-standard structures)
Full attribute-based access control (more flexible, very complex)

decisions.title: How do we version payroll rules so past calculations never change?
decisions.teaser: Tax law changes. Payroll rules change. But historical calculations must remain immutable.
decisions.answer: Every payroll configuration gets a version number and effective date. When you run payroll, the system locks in which rule version applies to that period. You can audit any past calculation by inspecting which rules were active at that time.
alternatives: Versioning in separate service (adds latency, operational complexity)
Bitemporal data model (most flexible, requires significant infrastructure)

callout.text: Architecture decisions should directly map to business constraints. Our multi-tenant layer exists because SMBs are price-sensitive, not because distributed systems are cool.

designDecision.title: Rules as First-Class Objects
designDecision.description: Rather than hardcoding payroll logic, we made tax/payroll rules into data entities. Each rule has a schema, validation, versioning, and an effective date. New compliance requirements don't require code deploys.
designDecision.why: Compliance requirements change 3-4 times yearly in Morocco. Code deployments for every change would be unsustainable. Rules as data gives us the agility we need.

constraints.title: Compliance changes constantly
constraints.description: Moroccan tax law updates multiple times per year. Every update ripples through payroll calculations, deductions, and reporting.

constraints.title: Currency precision matters
constraints.description: Working with Moroccan Dirhams and international conversions requires exact precision. Floating point errors are not acceptable.

constraints.title: Audit trail is non-negotiable
constraints.description: Every transaction must be auditable down to who changed what and when. For payroll, this is both legal requirement and customer expectation.

sections.title: What we chose NOT to do (and why)

tradeoffs.title: No real-time collaboration (yet)
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Eventual consistency model where multiple users can work on documents independently
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Can't show live cursor positions or simultaneous edits on the same form
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When we have more resources and when real-time sync becomes a customer pain point

tradeoffs.title: No mobile app at launch
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Responsive web app optimized for mobile browsers
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Native app experience, offline capability, tight OS integration
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: Once desktop product is stable and we see specific mobile-only workflows we can optimize

tradeoffs.title: No integration marketplace
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Custom API for integrations built case-by-case
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Integration partners have to come to us rather than self-serve
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: Once you have enough integration demand to justify building the platform

milestones.title: Architecture planning
milestones.description: Spent 6 weeks on system design, threat modeling, and compliance mapping. No code written yet.

milestones.title: Core data model
milestones.description: Built tenant isolation layer, user management, and audit infrastructure. All mutations flow through this.

milestones.title: Rules engine MVP
milestones.description: Implemented versioned rule system for payroll and tax calculations. First compliance configuration shipped.

milestones.title: First tenant onboarded
milestones.description: Real data flowing through the system. Discovered edge cases we never anticipated. Architecture held.

milestones.title: Ongoing improvements
milestones.description: Iterating on performance, adding new compliance requirements, building reporting layer.

reflections.insight: Compliance has to live in the data model, not the UI layer. Every business rule that can vary must be queryable and auditable from the database. This took us three months to understand.
reflections.transferable: Treat regulatory requirements as data schema problems, not application logic problems.

reflections.insight: Versioning is harder than you think. You need to version not just rules, but their dependencies and side effects. Simple versioning breaks down fast when rules interact.
reflections.transferable: If you're building a rules engine, invest early in versioning and testing. Don't retrofit it later.

reflections.insight: Multi-tenancy at the database layer was the right call for us. Yes, it requires discipline with query scoping, but the operational simplicity was worth it.
reflections.transferable: Don't separate databases per tenant unless you have a very specific reason. The ops cost often outweighs the isolation benefit.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: The rebuild has given us a solid foundation, but the work is far from finished. We're now focused on three areas:

items.title: Integration layer
items.description: Most of our customers use Generafi alongside other systems. Building clean APIs and webhooks is the next priority.

items.title: Advanced reporting
items.description: The audit log is comprehensive but raw. We need powerful, user-friendly reporting that lets SMBs answer their own questions.

items.title: Performance at scale
items.description: We're currently handling small-to-medium workloads. Preparing for larger customers means optimizing queries, caching strategies, and possibly rethinking some architectural choices.

problem: Moroccan SMBs need accounting and payroll software that reflects local compliance rules, audit expectations, and operating constraints.
solution: A compliance-first architecture with tenant-scoped data, versioned payroll rules, and operator workflows for review and approval.
role: Product architecture, domain modeling, and interface direction for the rebuild.
proof: The rebuild models payroll rules, tenant isolation, and audit events as first-class product infrastructure.
challenges: Designing permission boundaries for real SMB structures
learnings: Compliance belongs in the data model, not only the UI.
Auditability has to be designed before automation feels trustworthy.
